content:
It may be lagging while talking to the server. I find that reordering folders is very fast with Local Folders and sluggish with IMAP folders. But I don't recall it ever not working at all, I just sometimes have to be a little patient while things get in gear.

I kind of hate the folder ordering procedure. It's too fiddly and prone to error and more than once I've dropped a folder into another by accident, which can take a long time to recover from if it's a large IMAP folder.

This may or may not be helpful, but I discovered this recently: when you grab a folder, move it over to the right a bit, then move it between two adjacent folders, I see a tiny dot, just a pixel or so, which is the indicator that I can now drop it between them. If I see an adjacent folder surrounded by a box, it will go into that folder when I drop it. I don't see a "blue placement line" but that might be because of my color choices.

content:
By the way, consider using Local Folders. I find them to be indispensable. For example, suppose you discontinue an email address (or it gets taken away) and online access disappears. You can copy all messages from that gone-by account to Local Folders for archive and searching purposes. Or, if your email provider has quota limits, you can move the oldest ones to a local folder and free up space.

And back up your Thunderbird Profile regularly!

Maybe you're one of the many people who don't use and value old email like I do. For me, they are a searchable record of the past several decades of my on-line and "real" life.

Here's an article about certificates and what happens when they expire. It's written about Firefox, but it applies to Thunderbird, too.

https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/error-codes-secure-websites#w_when-you-cant-bypass-the-warning

The issue can come for a few different reasons, one of which can be your anti-virus software. Otherwise, the problem is most likely to be a mis-configured email server. Unless you have personal control over it, there's not much you can do about it but to contact their tech support.

Since the expiration date is today, maybe they are already working on it and if you give it some time and try again you may find it will have been fixed.

IMAP (Incoming): Incoming Server Type: IMAP Mail Server Incoming Server Name: imap.comcast.net Port: 993 User Name: <your Comcast email address> Connection Security: SSL/TLS Authentication method: Normal Password OR OAuth2

content:
You must be doing something different that I am, or there's something peculiar about your laptop. The attached animations shows me moving the window all over the screen and even offscreen. However, it turns out that this feature of Windows has a quirk: after you use Alt+Space, M, arrow keys to move the window, then hit Esc or Enter to stop moving, if you've moved the windows upwards, it snaps back down. If you have moved it left or right or down, it stays put, but up it snaps back. That makes it not so useful for getting at buttons along the bottom of the window.

Another way to get at the buttons is using the Tab key. Tab will cycle you through all the controls in order, including eventually the OK button. Shift+Tab will take you backwards.

Check out the Identities feature. It lets you save certain aspects of your messages, including signatures, as "Identities". Then, when you create a message or a response, you can choose from a drop-down list of the ones you have created. Here's an article about them:

https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/using-identities

By the way, any donation you might like to make would be appreciated and would help the project, but it's not expected. Thunderbird is free, open-source software, and there's no money needed to download, install, or use it.

content:
As I understand it, you had a backup of the profile, but when restoring, it would not work. Knowing the problem at the time, may have been useful. But that's history now. You deleted thousands of files related to thunderbird, but did not explain from where. That also may have been useful. So, you have a profile now with the restored addressbook, but not the messages. Here is my suggestion:

  1. set up the POP accounts, but keep offline. That is, do not allow accounts to retrieve mail.
  2. now, exit thunterbird and copy the backup of the profile\Mail folder to overlay whatever is there. The intent here is to keep the same popstate.dat intact so that, when you next retrieve mail, you will pick up from where you left off. On a restart, that should work. BUT, since we don't know why the original restore didn't work, I can't be sure.

If those suggested steps seem inappropriate, then more information is needed. Posting a screenshot of the Mail folder may prove helpful. Thank you.

NOTE: Have you considered using multiple profiles to allow use of multiple drives to minimize loss or backup/restore exposure? Not knowing your details, 280 gig is a lot of gig. :)

content:
First, I believe thunderbird to be stupid, and I say that as a compliment. It does not make decisions, but acts on what we users present to it. When you rightclick on unified folders and click properties and then click 'choose', do the other accounts appear in the list? If so, try putting tickmarks by those accounts. If you do not see those other accounts listed, then try this:

  1. click help>troubleshootinginformation
  2. scroll to 'profile folder' and click 'open folder'
  3. now, exit thunderbird
  4. you are now in the profile, click the Mail folder and you should see 'Smart Mailboxes' folder. Delete it.
  5. restart thunderbird and it will recreate the smart mailboxes automatically. Now, try again to set the accounts by rightclicking the unified folder, clicking properties and then clicking 'choose'

Per poter inquadrare il problema prova a fornire queste informazioni:

  1. Versione di Thunderbird
  2. Versione del sistema operativo
  3. Provider delle caselle email (Gmail, Libero, Hotmail, etc.)
  4. Tipologia di account (POP3, IMAP)
  5. Estensioni installate
  6. Messaggi di errore che vengono visualizzati durante l'utilizzo di Thunderbird
